title: Architecture of the CPU
content:
🧠 The Purpose of the CPU

The Central Processing Unit (CPU) is the brains of the computer. Its purpose is to execute instructions by carrying out the fetch-execute cycle — fetch an instruction from memory, decode it, then execute it.

flowchart TD
    PC["📍 Program Counter
(PC)"]:::addr -->|"① Fetch
address"| MAR["📮 Memory Address
Register (MAR)"]:::addr
    MAR -->|"② Read
memory"| RAM["💾 RAM"]:::mem
    RAM -->|"③ Return
data"| MDR["📋 Memory Data
Register (MDR)"]:::data
    MDR -->|"④ Transfer"| CIR["🧮 Current Instruction
Register (CIR)"]:::ctrl
    CIR -->|"⑤ Decode"| CU["🎛️ Control Unit
(CU)"]:::ctrl
    CU -->|"⑥ Execute"| ALU["🔢 ALU"]:::alu
    CU -->|"⑥ Execute"| ACC["📊 Accumulator
(ACC)"]:::data

    classDef addr fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#6d28d9,color:#fff
    classDef mem fill:#2563eb,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#fff
    classDef data fill:#16a34a,stroke:#15803d,color:#fff
    classDef ctrl fill:#d97706,stroke:#b45309,color:#fff
    classDef alu fill:#e11d48,stroke:#be123c,color:#fff

⚙️ The Fetch-Execute Cycle

The CPU continuously repeats three steps:

  1. 🚀 Fetch: Get the next instruction from memory (RAM) using the address in the Program Counter (PC)
  2. 🧩 Decode: Work out what the instruction means (done by the Control Unit)
  3. ⚡ Execute: Carry out the instruction (e.g. add two numbers using the ALU)
📌 Exam Tip: A 3-mark question: "Describe the fetch-execute cycle." Mention all three steps: fetch (get instruction from RAM), decode (CU interprets it), execute (CU/ALU carries it out). Use at least one register name (PC, MAR, MDR, CIR) for full marks.

🔧 CPU Components

ComponentFunction🔑 Role
ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit)Performs arithmetic (+, −, ×, ÷) and logical (AND, OR, NOT) operations🔢 Does all the maths and logic
CU (Control Unit)Coordinates all CPU operations. Decodes instructions, controls data flow🎛️ The conductor of the orchestra
CacheHigh-speed memory inside the CPU that stores frequently used data/instructions⚡ Faster than RAM, smaller in size
RegistersVery fast, small memory locations inside the CPU for specific purposes📍 Temporary storage for data/addresses

📝 Key Von Neumann Registers

RegisterFull NamePurpose
PCProgram Counter📍 Holds the address of the next instruction to fetch
MARMemory Address Register📮 Stores the address in RAM that the CPU needs to read/write
MDRMemory Data Register📋 Holds the actual data fetched from memory or waiting to be written
ACCAccumulator📊 Stores the result of ALU calculations
💡 Key Difference: The MAR stores an address (a memory location). The MDR stores the actual data itself. Don't confuse these two — it's a classic exam trap!

title: CPU Performance
content:
⚡ CPU Performance

Not all Central Processing Units (CPUs) perform at the same speed. The overall speed and performance of a CPU are determined by three main hardware characteristics: clock speed, cache size, and the number of cores.

flowchart TD
    Perf["⚡ CPU Performance Factors"]:::title
    Perf --> CS["🕒 Clock Speed
Hertz / Cycle Rate"]:::blue
    Perf --> Cores["🧠 Cores
Parallel processing units"]:::green
    Perf --> Cache["💾 Cache Size
Ultra-fast temporary memory"]:::amber

    classDef title fill:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3
    classDef blue fill:#2563eb,color:#ffffff
    classDef green fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    classDef amber fill:#d97706,color:#ffffff

🕒 Clock Speed

The CPU has an internal clock that sends signals at a regular frequency. The clock speed is the number of pulses sent per second, measured in Hertz (Hz). Modern CPUs are measured in Gigahertz (GHz), where 1 GHz means 1 billion clock cycles per second.

During each clock cycle, the CPU performs one Fetch-Decode-Execute instruction phase. Therefore, a higher clock speed means more instructions can be processed per second, making programs run faster.

🧠 Number of Cores

A core is an independent processing unit within the CPU. A CPU with multiple cores (e.g., dual-core, quad-core) can execute multiple instructions at the same time. This is called parallel processing.

However, doubling the number of cores does not always double the performance. Some software is not designed to use multiple cores (it cannot divide its tasks), and coordinating tasks between cores creates communication overhead.

💾 Cache Size

Cache is extremely fast memory built directly into the CPU chip. It holds frequently used instructions and data. Because it is much closer to the CPU registers than RAM, the CPU can access it almost instantly.

A larger cache size means more data can be stored there, reducing the number of times the CPU has to wait to retrieve data from the much slower RAM.

⚖️ Performance Characteristics Comparison

CharacteristicHow it affects performanceLimitations
Clock SpeedMore instruction cycles executed per second.Generates more heat and increases power consumption.
Number of CoresAllows parallel processing (multiple tasks at once).Requires software optimization; communication overhead between cores.
Cache SizeFaster access to frequently used data than RAM.Very expensive to manufacture; limited capacity.
📌 Exam Tip: A classic 3-mark question: "Explain how the number of cores affects CPU performance." State: 1) A core is an independent processing unit, 2) more cores allow multiple instructions to be processed simultaneously (parallel processing), 3) which speeds up performance, but 4) this depends on software compatibility and creates communication overhead.
⚠️ Common Mistake: Confusing clock speed with core count, or assuming that a 3 GHz quad-core processor is simply 4 times faster than a 3 GHz single-core processor. Performance gains are rarely linear.

title: Embedded Systems
content:
🔌 What is an Embedded System?

An embedded system is a computer system built into a larger device to perform a dedicated function. Unlike a general-purpose computer (laptop or desktop), it has a single purpose.

💡 Key Insight: Embedded systems are everywhere — most electronic devices contain one. The key difference from a general-purpose computer is that an embedded system is dedicated to a single task, making it cheaper, smaller, and more power-efficient.
flowchart LR
    subgraph Embedded["🔌 Embedded System"]
        ES1["🎯 Single purpose"]
        ES2["⚙️ Limited hardware"]
        ES3["🔋 Low power"]
        ES4["💰 Low cost"]
    end
    subgraph General["💻 General-Purpose"]
        GP1["📦 Runs many programs"]
        GP2["🧩 Expandable hardware"]
        GP3["🔌 Higher power"]
        GP4["💵 Higher cost"]
    end

    classDef emb fill:#2563eb,color:#ffffff
    classDef gen fill:#d97706,color:#ffffff

📋 Characteristics

  • Dedicated to one specific task
  • Limited processing power and memory (just enough for the job)
  • Low power consumption (often battery-powered)
  • Cheaper to manufacture than a full computer
  • Usually runs a simple operating system or no OS at all (bare-metal firmware)
  • Difficult or impossible to upgrade or reprogram

Microcontrollers vs. Microprocessors

Embedded systems are usually controlled by a microcontroller (MCU). A microcontroller has the CPU, RAM, ROM, and input/output ports all integrated onto a single silicon chip. This differs from a general-purpose microprocessor (MPU), which has a separate CPU chip and requires external memory chips and controllers.

Because the memory and peripherals are on-chip, microcontrollers are compact, cheaper to produce, and use very little power, making them ideal for small, portable, or mass-produced items.

🔧 Real-World Examples

  • Washing machines: Controls the wash cycle, temperature, and spin speed.
  • Microwave ovens: Manages cooking time and power levels.
  • Anti-lock Braking Systems (ABS): Monitors wheel speed sensors and dynamically adjusts brake pressure to prevent skidding.
  • Smart thermostats: Learns user habits, monitors temperature, and controls heating/cooling remotely.
  • Fitness trackers: Uses accelerometers to count steps and monitors heart rate via optical sensors, saving battery life.

⚖️ Embedded vs General-Purpose

Embedded SystemGeneral-Purpose Computer
Single purposeVersatile — runs many different programs
Limited hardware (just enough)Expandable hardware (add RAM, GPU, etc.)
Low costHigher cost
Low power consumptionHigher power consumption
Difficult to upgradeEasy to upgrade
📌 Exam Tip: A common question: "Give two examples of embedded systems and explain why each uses one instead of a general-purpose computer." Mention cost (cheaper), power consumption (battery life), and size (fits inside the device).

title: Primary Storage (Memory)
content:
💾 Primary Storage (Memory)

Primary storage is memory that the CPU can access directly. It's fast but volatile — data is lost when the power is turned off.

🧠 RAM — Random Access Memory

  • ⚡ Volatile — loses data when power is off
  • 🚀 Fast read/write speeds
  • 📦 Stores currently running programs and data
  • 🧩 Two types: DRAM (dynamic) and SRAM (static, faster but more expensive)

💿 ROM — Read Only Memory

  • 🔒 Non-volatile — retains data without power
  • 📝 Stores the BIOS/UEFI firmware that boots the computer
  • 🔐 Cannot be easily modified (read-only)

⚡ Memory Hierarchy

flowchart TD
    TOP["🏆 Fastest, Smallest, Most Expensive"]:::top
    R1["📋 CPU Registers
(~1 cycle, bytes)"]:::fast
    R2["⚡ Cache L1 / L2 / L3
(~10 cycles, KB–MB)"]:::fast
    R3["💾 RAM (Main Memory)
(~100 cycles, GB)"]:::mid
    R4["💿 Virtual Memory
(on SSD/HDD, ~10M cycles, GB)"]:::slow
    BOT["🐢 Slowest, Largest, Cheapest"]:::slow

    TOP --> R1
    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> R3
    R3 --> R4
    R4 --> BOT

    classDef top fill:#6366f1,stroke:#4f46e5,color:#ffffff
    classDef fast fill:#16a34a,stroke:#15803d,color:#ffffff
    classDef mid fill:#2563eb,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#ffffff
    classDef slow fill:#d97706,stroke:#b45309,color:#ffffff
💡 Key Insight: The memory hierarchy is a trade-off between speed, size, and cost. Faster memory is more expensive, so we use small amounts of fast memory (cache) backed by large amounts of slower memory (RAM).

📊 RAM vs ROM

FeatureRAMROM
💨 SpeedFastSlower than RAM
⚡ Volatile?Yes — loses data on power offNo — retains data permanently
✏️ Writable?Yes — read and writeNo — read only (mostly)
🔧 PurposeRunning programs and dataBoot instructions (BIOS/UEFI)
📏 SizeTypically 4–32 GBTypically 4–16 MB
📌 Exam Tip: A common 3-mark question: "Explain why a computer needs both RAM and ROM." Key points: RAM is volatile, stores running programs/data. ROM is non-volatile, stores the boot-up instructions. Both are needed for the computer to function.

🔄 Virtual Memory

When RAM is full, the OS uses part of the secondary storage (SSD/HDD) as virtual memory. It swaps data between RAM and storage as needed. This is much slower than real RAM because storage drives are orders of magnitude slower than RAM chips.

  • 🔀 Allows more programs to run than RAM alone can hold
  • 🐢 Dramatically slows performance when heavily used (thrashing)
  • 📉 Modern SSDs help, but still much slower than RAM

title: Secondary Storage
content:
💾 Why Do Computers Need Secondary Storage?

Secondary storage is non-volatile — it retains data when the power is off. It is used to store files, programs, and the operating system for the long term.

Types of Secondary Storage

💿 Optical Storage (CD, DVD, Blu-ray)

  • Data is read/written using a laser beam
  • Pits and lands on the disc surface represent binary data
  • Cheap to produce, portable, but slow and low capacity
  • Used for: music CDs, movie DVDs, game discs

🧲 Magnetic Storage (Hard Disk Drives — HDD)

  • Data is stored on spinning platters coated with magnetic material
  • A read/write head moves across the platter to access data
  • Large capacity for low cost — common in desktop PCs and servers
  • Slower than SSDs, vulnerable to physical damage (moving parts)

⚡ Solid State Storage (SSDs, USB flash drives, memory cards)

  • Data is stored on flash memory chips — no moving parts
  • Much faster than HDDs and optical storage
  • More durable (no moving parts to break)
  • More expensive per gigabyte than HDDs
  • Used for: smartphones, tablets, ultrabooks, cameras, USB drives
flowchart TD
    ST["📦 Choosing Storage"]:::title
    ST --> Speed["⚡ Speed
SSD > HDD > Optical"]:::speed
    ST --> Capacity["📏 Capacity
HDD > SSD > Optical"]:::cap
    ST --> Cost["💷 Cost per GB
HDD < Optical < SSD"]:::cost
    ST --> Durability["🛡️ Durability
SSD > Optical > HDD"]:::dur

    classDef title fill:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3
    classDef speed fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    classDef cap fill:#2563eb,color:#ffffff
    classDef cost fill:#d97706,color:#ffffff
    classDef dur fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff

Choosing the Right Storage

FactorWhat It Means
CapacityHow much data can it hold? (GB / TB)
SpeedHow fast can data be read/written?
PortabilityHow easy is it to carry around?
DurabilityHow resistant is it to physical damage?
ReliabilityHow likely is it to fail?
CostHow much per gigabyte?
💡 Key Insight: There is no single "best" storage type — each has trade-offs. SSDs are fast and durable but expensive per GB. HDDs offer huge capacity for less money. Optical discs are cheap to produce and portable but slow. Choose based on: speed vs capacity vs cost vs portability.

Example scenario: For a portable music player, you would choose solid state (portable, durable, low power). For a home media server, a large HDD is better value (high capacity, low cost per GB).

📌 Exam Tip: A common 4-mark question: "Compare optical, magnetic, and solid state storage." Structure your answer by speed (SSD fastest, HDD mid, optical slowest), capacity (HDD highest, SSD mid, optical lowest), durability (SSD best — no moving parts), and cost per GB (HDD cheapest, optical mid, SSD most expensive).

Storage Type Comparison

Optical Storage Laser reads pits and lands
Data is read/written using a laser beam. Pits and lands on the disc surface represent binary 0s and 1s. CD (~700 MB), DVD (~4.7 GB), Blu-ray (~25 GB).
Speed Slow
Capacity Low
Durability Medium
Cost per GB Cheap

💻 Binary — The Language of Computers

Computers use binary (base-2) to represent all data. Everything — numbers, text, images, sound — is ultimately stored as a sequence of 0s and 1s.

💡 Remember: 1 byte = 8 bits. In the OCR exam, 1 KB = 1,000 bytes (decimal). A megabyte is roughly one 3-minute MP3 song. A gigabyte is about a full HD movie. These comparisons help you estimate file sizes in exam questions.

🧮 Calculating File Sizes

  • Sound file: sample rate (Hz) × duration (seconds) × bit depth
  • Image file: colour depth (bits per pixel) × image height (px) × image width (px)
  • Text file: bits per character × number of characters

✏️ Example Calculation

A 10-second audio clip with a sample rate of 44,100 Hz and 16-bit depth:
44,100 × 10 × 16 = 7,056,000 bits = 882,000 bytes ≈ 882 KB

📌 Exam Tip: You may be asked to calculate a file size. Remember: Sound = sample rate × duration × bit depth. Image = colour depth × height × width. Convert your answer to sensible units (bits → bytes → KB).

title: Data Representation & Compression
content:
🔢 Numbers — Binary and Hexadecimal

🔢 Denary to Binary

Each bit represents a power of 2: 128, 64, 32, 16, 8, 4, 2, 1 (for 8 bits). To convert 173 to binary: subtract each power of 2 from left to right — place a 1 if it fits, otherwise 0.

173 = 128 + 32 + 8 + 4 + 1 = 10101101

➕ Binary Addition

Add binary numbers column by column, carrying 1 when the result is 2 or more. An overflow error occurs when the result needs more bits than available (e.g., adding two 8-bit numbers that need 9 bits).

💡 Key Point: One hex digit = 4 bits (one nibble). Hex is shorter and more human-readable than binary. 0xFF = 255 in denary. Hex is used for memory addresses, colour codes, and debugging.
flowchart LR
    BIN["Binary
10101101"]:::bin -->|"→"| HEX["Hex
AD"]:::hex
    HEX -->|"→"| DEN["Denary
173"]:::den
    B2["128 · 64 · 32 · 16
8 · 4 · 2 · 1"]:::bits
    B2 -->|"↓"| B3["1 · 0 · 1 · 0
1 · 1 · 0 · 1"]:::bits

    classDef bin fill:#2563eb,color:#ffffff
    classDef hex fill:#d97706,color:#ffffff
    classDef den fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    classDef bits fill:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff

🔣 Hexadecimal

Base-16 using digits 0-9 and letters A-F. Used because it's more compact than binary. One hex digit = 4 bits (one nibble).

FF = 255 in denary. F = 15.

⬅️ Binary Shifts

Left shift: Multiplies the number by 2 for each shift (fills with 0s on the right).
Right shift: Divides the number by 2 for each shift (discards bits on the right).

🔤 Characters — ASCII and Unicode

Character sets map characters to binary codes. ASCII uses 7 bits (128 characters), while Unicode uses up to 32 bits (over 1 million characters). Unicode can represent characters from most written languages.

🖼️ Images

Images are stored as a grid of pixels. Each pixel stores a binary colour code. Colour depth = bits per pixel (e.g., 24-bit = 16.7 million colours). Resolution = width × height in pixels. Metadata stores extra info like image dimensions and camera settings.

🔊 Sound

Analogue sound waves are sampled at regular intervals to convert them to digital. Sample rate (Hz) = number of samples per second. Bit depth = bits per sample. Higher sample rate and bit depth = better quality but larger file.

title: Networks and Topologies
content:
🌐 Types of Network

📡 LAN — Local Area Network

A network that covers a small geographical area (e.g., a home, school, or office building). All hardware is owned by the organisation that runs the network.

🌍 WAN — Wide Area Network

A network that covers a large geographical area (e.g., cities, countries). The Internet is the largest WAN. WANs are often made up of multiple LANs connected together using leased lines or satellite links.

📌 Exam Tip: The key difference is geographical area, not size. A WAN connects LANs across cities/countries. The Internet is the ultimate example.

📊 Factors Affecting Network Performance

  • ⚡ Bandwidth: The maximum data transferred per second. More bandwidth = faster network.
  • 📱 Number of devices: More devices = less bandwidth per device = slower speeds.
  • 📏 Physical distance: Longer distances = weaker signals and more latency.
  • 🚧 Interference: Wireless networks affected by walls, electronics, and radio signals.

🏢 Client-Server vs 🤝 Peer-to-Peer

Client-ServerPeer-to-Peer
🎯 Central server manages resources and security🔗 No central server — each device is equal
🔐 Users log in to access shared files📁 Each device shares directly with others
🛡️ Better security and backup⚠️ No central management or backup
🏫 Used in schools, businesses🏠 Used for small home networks or file sharing

🔧 Network Hardware

  • 🔀 Switch: Connects devices on a LAN, sends data only to the correct device
  • 🌉 Router: Connects different networks (e.g., home LAN to Internet)
  • 📶 Wireless Access Point (WAP): Allows wireless devices to connect to a wired network
  • 💳 NIC: Built into each device to connect to a network (wired or wireless)
  • 🔌 Transmission media: Physical cables (copper, fibre optic) or wireless signals

⭐ Star vs 🔀 Mesh Topology

flowchart TD
    subgraph STAR["⭐ Star Topology"]
        SW["🔀 Central Switch"]:::switch
        S1["Device A"]:::device --- SW
        S2["Device B"]:::device --- SW
        S3["Device C"]:::device --- SW
        S4["Device D"]:::device --- SW
    end

    subgraph MESH["🔀 Mesh Topology"]
        M1["Device A"]:::device --- M2["Device B"]:::device
        M1 --- M3["Device C"]:::device
        M1 --- M4["Device D"]:::device
        M2 --- M3
        M2 --- M4
        M3 --- M4
    end

    classDef switch fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#6d28d9,color:#ffffff
    classDef device fill:#2563eb,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#ffffff

Star: All devices connect to a central switch/router. Easy to manage, but if the central switch fails, the whole network goes down.

Mesh: Every device connects to every other device. Very resilient (no single point of failure) but expensive and complex to set up.

💡 Key Insight: Most home networks use a star topology (all devices → router). The Internet uses a hybrid topology — a mix of star and mesh.

title: Wired and Wireless Networks, Protocols and Layers
content:
🌐 Modes of Connection

flowchart LR
    Wired["🔌 Wired"]:::wired
    Wireless["📡 Wireless"]:::wireless

    Wired --> WF["✅ Faster & more reliable"]
    Wired --> WS["✅ More secure"]
    Wired --> WC["❌ Needs physical cables"]

    Wireless --> WL["✅ Convenient, mobile"]
    Wireless --> WSp["❌ Slower, interference"]
    Wireless --> WSe["❌ Less secure"]

    classDef wired fill:#2563eb,color:#ffffff
    classDef wireless fill:#d97706,color:#ffffff

🔌 Wired (Ethernet)

  • Faster and more reliable than wireless
  • More secure — data doesn't travel through the air
  • Less interference from other devices
  • Requires physical cables — less convenient for mobile devices

📡 Wireless (Wi-Fi & Bluetooth)

  • Convenient for mobile devices — no cables
  • Easier interference (walls, other networks)
  • Slower and less secure than wired connections
  • Wi-Fi: Used for network access over medium range (tens of metres)
  • Bluetooth: Short-range, lower power, used for peripherals (headphones, keyboards)

🔒 Encryption

Data sent across networks is encrypted (scrambled) so that even if intercepted, it can't be read without the encryption key.

💡 Remember: IP addresses identify devices across networks (like a postal address). MAC addresses identify devices on a local network (like a person's name). Both are needed for data to reach the right device.

📇 IP and MAC Addressing

  • IP address: A logical address assigned to a device on a network. IPv4 = 32-bit (e.g., 192.168.0.38). IPv6 = 128-bit.
  • MAC address: A unique hardware address burned into each NIC. Used within a LAN to deliver data to the right device.

📋 Key Protocols

ProtocolPurpose
TCP/IPGoverning protocol of the Internet — splits data into packets and ensures reliable delivery
HTTPUsed to transfer web pages between servers and browsers
HTTPSSecure version of HTTP — data is encrypted using TLS/SSL
FTPUsed to transfer files between computers (upload/download)
POP / IMAPUsed to receive emails (POP downloads, IMAP syncs across devices)
SMTPUsed to send emails

🧅 The Concept of Layers

Network communication is split into layers. Each layer handles a specific part of the communication process (e.g., one layer handles physical cabling, another handles error checking).

Benefits of layers:

  • Different manufacturers' hardware/software can work together (interoperability)
  • Each layer can be developed independently
  • If one layer changes, the others don't need to change
📌 Exam Tip: A common 4-mark question: "Explain the benefits of using layers in networking." Key points: each layer can be developed independently, different manufacturers can interoperate, changes to one layer don't affect others, and it breaks a complex problem into manageable parts.

title: Threats to Computer Systems and Networks
content:
🛡️ Forms of Attack

    Th --> Mal["🦠 Malware
Virus, Worm, Trojan,
Ransomware"]:::mal
    Th --> Soc["🎣 Social Eng.
Phishing,
Tricking users"]:::soc
    Th --> Brut["🔑 Brute Force
Try all passwords
until one works"]:::brut
    Th --> DoS["💥 DoS/DDoS

with traffic"]:::dos
    Th --> Inter["👀 Interception
Sniff data
on network"]:::inter

Malicious code
in form fields"]:::sql

    classDef soc fill:#d97706,color:#ffffff
    classDef brut fill:#2563eb,color:#ffffff
    classDef dos fill:#9333ea,color:#ffffff
    classDef inter fill:#0891b2,color:#ffffff
    classDef sql fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff

🦠 Malware

Malicious software designed to harm a computer system. Types include viruses (self-replicating), worms (spread across networks), trojans (disguised as legitimate software), and ransomware (encrypts files and demands payment).

💡 Key Insight: Humans are often the weakest link in cybersecurity. Social engineering exploits human psychology rather than technical vulnerabilities. Never click links in unexpected emails — this is how most cyber attacks begin.

🎣 Social Engineering

Manipulating people into revealing confidential information. Phishing is the most common form — fake emails or websites that look genuine to trick you into entering passwords or bank details. Humans are often described as the 'weakest link' in security.

🔑 Brute-Force Attacks

Automated software tries every possible password combination until it finds the right one. Using strong, long passwords makes this type of attack impractical.

💥 Denial of Service (DoS) Attacks

Overwhelming a server with so much traffic that it becomes unavailable to legitimate users. A Distributed DoS (DDoS) attack uses many infected computers (a botnet) to launch the attack simultaneously.

👀 Data Interception and Theft

Intercepting data as it travels across a network. This is why encryption is important — even if data is intercepted, it can't be read.

💉 SQL Injection

Inserting malicious SQL code into a web form input field. If a website doesn't properly validate user input, the attacker can trick the database into revealing or deleting data.

📌 Exam Tip: For a 4-mark "explain" question about a type of attack, structure your answer as: what it is (definition), how it works (mechanism), what it targets, and how to prevent it.

Interactive Threat Explorer

Set a 4-digit PIN:
Progress:
Attempts: 0 Ready
How Brute Force Works: Automated software tries every possible password combination (0000, 0001, 0002...) until it finds the right one. A 4-digit PIN has only 10,000 combinations — a computer can crack this in seconds. Using longer passwords with letters, numbers, and symbols makes brute-force attacks take years instead of seconds.
📧 security@bank0famerica-secure.com
Subject: Urgent: Your account has been compromised!
Dear valued customer,

We detected suspicious activity on your account. Click the link below to verify your details immediately or your account will be suspended.

https://bank0famerica-secure.com/verify
How Phishing Works: Fake emails or websites trick users into revealing passwords, bank details, or personal data. Red flags: urgent/threatening language, misspelt URLs (bank0famerica instead of bankofamerica), generic greetings, and demands to click links.
Server traffic (requests per second):
Normal: 30 req/s Total: 30 req/s Server: Online
How DDoS Works: A botnet of infected computers sends massive amounts of traffic to a server, overwhelming its capacity. Legitimate users cannot access the service. Normal traffic gets drowned out by the flood of attack traffic.
Login form — try an SQL injection:
Username:
Password:
How SQL Injection Works: An attacker types SQL code into input fields instead of expected data. If the website does not validate inputs, the SQL code runs on the database — potentially revealing, modifying, or deleting data. Parameterised queries and input validation prevent this.

title: Identifying and Preventing Vulnerabilities
content:
🔐 Prevention Methods

flowchart TD
    Def["🔐 Defence Layers"]:::title
    Def --> PT["🔍 Penetration Testing
Ethical hackers find weaknesses"]:::blue
    Def --> AV["🛡️ Anti-Malware
Scans for known threats"]:::green
    Def --> FW["🧱 Firewall
Blocks unauthorised traffic"]:::amber
    Def --> UAL["👤 User Access Levels
Least privilege principle"]:::purple
    Def --> Aut["✅ Authentication
Passwords, 2FA, biometrics"]:::cyan

    classDef title fill:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3
    classDef blue fill:#2563eb,color:#ffffff
    classDef green fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    classDef amber fill:#d97706,color:#ffffff
    classDef purple fill:#9333ea,color:#ffffff
    classDef cyan fill:#0891b2,color:#ffffff

Penetration Testing

Ethical hackers are employed to deliberately try to break into a system to find security weaknesses before real attackers do. Also known as 'pen testing'.

💡 Remember: Penetration testing ("pen testing") is authorised hacking to find vulnerabilities before real attackers do. White-box testing gives the tester full system knowledge; black-box testing gives none — both are valuable.

Anti-Malware Software

Scans files and programs for known malware signatures. Detects and removes viruses, trojans, ransomware, and other malicious software.

Firewalls

Monitors incoming and outgoing network traffic. Blocks unauthorised connections based on a set of security rules. Can be hardware (a physical device) or software (a program on your computer).

User Access Levels

Not everyone needs access to everything. User accounts can have different permission levels (e.g., standard user vs administrator). This limits what an attacker can access if they compromise a standard user account.

Passwords

Strong passwords (long, complex, unique) are a basic but vital defence. Combined with other methods like two-factor authentication (2FA), they significantly improve security.

Encryption

Encrypting data means even if it's intercepted or stolen, it can't be read without the decryption key. Used for both data at rest (stored files) and data in transit (network traffic).

Physical Security

Locking server rooms, using CCTV, and controlling physical access to devices. Sometimes the simplest measures are the most effective.

How They Map to Threats

ThreatPrevention
MalwareAnti-malware software, firewalls
Phishing (social engineering)User training, awareness
Brute-force attacksStrong passwords, account lockouts
DoS attacksFirewalls, traffic filtering
Data interceptionEncryption (HTTPS, VPNs)
SQL injectionInput validation, parameterised queries
📌 Exam Tip: A common question: "Explain the difference between a virus and a worm." Virus: attaches to a file, needs human action to spread. Worm: self-replicating, spreads across networks automatically without human intervention.

Security Measures — Identify the Defence

Firewall
Monitors and controls incoming and outgoing network traffic based on security rules. Can block unauthorised access while allowing legitimate traffic.
Prevents: Unauthorised network access, hacking attempts, malware communication

title: Operating Systems
content:
💻 What Does an Operating System Do?

The Operating System (OS) is the most important software on a computer. It manages the hardware and provides a platform for applications to run on. Examples: Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, Android.

flowchart TD
    OS["💻 Operating System"]:::title
    OS --> MM["🧠 Memory Management
Allocates RAM to programs"]:::blue
    OS --> FM["📁 File Management
Organises, saves, retrieves files"]:::green
    OS --> PM["🖨️ Peripheral Mgmt
Controls printers, keyboards, etc."]:::amber
    OS --> UI["🖥️ User Interface
GUI or command-line"]:::purple
    OS --> Sec["🔒 Security
User accounts, permissions"]:::cyan
    OS --> Multi["📊 Multitasking
Runs multiple programs at once"]:::red

    classDef title fill:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3
    classDef blue fill:#2563eb,color:#ffffff
    classDef green fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    classDef amber fill:#d97706,color:#ffffff
    classDef purple fill:#9333ea,color:#ffffff
    classDef cyan fill:#0891b2,color:#ffffff
    classDef red fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
💡 Key Insight: The operating system is the most important software on a computer. Without it, applications cannot run, hardware won't work together, and the user has no way to interact with the machine. Every general-purpose computer needs an OS.

Key Functions

1. User Interface

Provides a way for users to interact with the computer. Can be a Graphical User Interface (GUI) with windows and icons, a Command Line Interface (CLI) with text commands, or a touch-based interface on phones/tablets.

2. Memory Management and Multitasking

The OS allocates memory (RAM) to running programs. When multiple programs are running, the OS manages which program gets access to memory and processor time. This is how multitasking works — the OS rapidly switches between programs so they appear to run simultaneously.

3. Peripheral Management and Drivers

Manages input/output devices like keyboards, mice, printers, and monitors. The OS uses device drivers — special software that translates generic OS commands into specific instructions for each hardware device.

4. User Management

Supports multiple user accounts with different permissions. Allows setting up new users, managing passwords, and controlling what each user can access.

5. File Management

Organises files into folders, handles file naming, moving, copying, deleting, and saving. Also manages file permissions and disk space.

📌 Exam Tip: A 3-mark question: "State three functions of an operating system." Common answers: memory management (allocates RAM to programs), file management (organises stored data), peripheral management (controls input/output devices), user interface (provides a way to interact with the computer), security (manages user accounts and permissions).

Operating System Functions Explorer

Memory Management
Manages the allocation and de-allocation of RAM between running programs. Uses virtual memory to extend available memory when RAM is full. Prevents programs from interfering with each other's memory.
Analogy: A librarian allocating study spaces — decides who gets a desk (RAM), who shares, and who must wait or use the overflow room (virtual memory).

title: Utility Software
content:
🛠️ Purpose of Utility Software

Utility software performs housekeeping tasks that help maintain, optimise, and secure the computer system. They usually come bundled with the operating system but can also be installed separately.

flowchart LR
    Util["🛠️ Utility Software"]:::title
    Util --> AV2["🛡️ Antivirus
Detects & removes malware"]:::red
    Util --> Defrag["🧩 Defragmenter
Reorganises HDD files"]:::blue
    Util --> Comp["🗜️ Compression
Reduces file size"]:::green
    Util --> Backup["💾 Backup
Copies files for safety"]:::amber
    Util --> Disk["🧹 Disk Cleanup
Removes unnecessary files"]:::purple

    classDef title fill:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3
    classDef red fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
    classDef blue fill:#2563eb,color:#ffffff
    classDef green fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    classDef amber fill:#d97706,color:#ffffff
    classDef purple fill:#9333ea,color:#ffffff
💡 Remember: Utility software helps maintain the computer system — it's not essential for the computer to run (unlike the OS), but it keeps everything working efficiently. Common examples: antivirus, disk defragmenter, file compression, backup software.

Types of Utility Software

Encryption Software

Scrambles data so it can only be read by someone with the correct decryption key. Used to protect sensitive files and folders, especially on portable devices that could be lost or stolen.

Defragmentation Software

Only needed on magnetic hard drives (HDDs). Over time, files become fragmented — split into pieces scattered across the disk. Defragmentation reorganises these pieces so they are stored contiguously (in one place), which speeds up file access.

Note: SSDs don't need defragmentation and it can actually reduce their lifespan.

Data Compression Software

Reduces the size of files to save storage space and make them faster to transfer. Creates compressed archives like .zip or .rar files. Can use either lossy or lossless compression.

Why Utility Software Is Needed

Although the OS handles basic operations, utility software provides additional tools that improve system performance, security, or manageability. The OS can get by without them, but the system would run less efficiently and be less secure.

📌 Exam Tip: A 2-mark question: "What is the purpose of a disk defragmenter?" Answer: It reorganises files stored on a hard disk so that fragments of the same file are stored contiguously (next to each other), reducing the time the read/write head spends moving to access the file.

Utility Software Explorer

Antivirus Software
Scans files and programs for known malware signatures. Uses heuristic analysis to detect unknown or variant threats. Quarantines or deletes infected files. Runs regular system scans and real-time protection.
Why needed: Protects against viruses, worms, trojans, ransomware, and spyware. Essential for any computer connected to the internet.

title: Ethical, Legal, Cultural and Environmental Impacts
content:
⚖️ Impacts of Digital Technology

Digital technology affects society in many ways. You need to be able to discuss these issues and give examples.

flowchart TD
    Impact["⚖️ Areas of Impact"]:::title
    Impact --> Eth["🤔 Ethical
Privacy, AI bias,
automation, censorship"]:::blue
    Impact --> Leg["📜 Legal
Computer Misuse Act,
Data Protection, Copyright"]:::green
    Impact --> Cult["🌍 Cultural
Digital divide,
global homogenisation"]:::amber
    Impact --> Env["🌱 Environmental
E-waste, energy use,
rare earth mining"]:::red

    classDef title fill:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3
    classDef blue fill:#2563eb,color:#ffffff
    classDef green fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    classDef amber fill:#d97706,color:#ffffff
    classDef red fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
💡 Key Insight: The Computer Misuse Act (1990) makes it illegal to: (1) access a computer without permission, (2) access with intent to commit further crimes, (3) unauthorised modification of data (viruses, deleting files). The Data Protection Act governs how personal data must be handled.

Ethical Issues

  • Privacy: How much personal data should companies collect? Should governments have backdoor access to encrypted data?
  • AI and automation: Should AI make life-or-death decisions (e.g., self-driving cars)?
  • Censorship: Who decides what content is acceptable online?

Legal Issues

  • Copyright: Is it acceptable to download copyrighted music/movies without paying?
  • Cybercrime: Hacking, identity theft, and online fraud are illegal
  • Digital divide: Should internet access be a legal right?

Cultural Issues

  • Social media: Effects on mental health, spread of misinformation, echo chambers
  • Globalisation: Digital technology connects people worldwide but can threaten local cultures
  • Accessibility: Technology should be designed for everyone, including people with disabilities

Environmental Issues

  • E-waste: Discarded electronics contain toxic materials and fill landfills
  • Energy consumption: Data centres and cryptocurrency mining use enormous amounts of electricity
  • Manufacturing: Rare earth minerals for electronics often come from conflict zones or environmentally damaging mining operations

⚖️ Relevant Legislation

Data Protection Act 2018

Controls how personal data is collected, stored, and used. Individuals have the right to see what data is held about them and can request its deletion.

Computer Misuse Act 1990

Makes it illegal to access a computer system without authorisation (hacking), access with intent to commit further crimes, or modify data without permission.

Copyright, Designs and Patents Act 1988

Protects intellectual property — it's illegal to copy, distribute, or use copyrighted material without permission (e.g., downloading movies, copying software).

Software Licences

  • Open source: Source code is freely available. Users can view, modify, and distribute the software (e.g., Linux, Firefox). Usually free.
  • Proprietary: Source code is kept secret. Users purchase a licence to use the software but cannot modify it (e.g., Microsoft Windows, Adobe Photoshop).
📌 Exam Tip: A 6-mark discuss question: "Discuss the ethical and environmental impacts of technology." Structure: Environmental (e-waste, energy consumption, rare earth mining vs remote working reduces travel) and Ethical (privacy, job automation, digital divide, censorship). Conclude with a balanced judgement.

Ethical, Legal & Cultural Impact — Scenario Matcher

A social media company collects user data and sells it to advertisers without explicit user consent.
Key Laws for GCSE: Data Protection Act (2018) — controls how personal data is used. Computer Misuse Act (1990) — unauthorised access to computer material. Copyright, Designs and Patents Act (1988) — protects intellectual property. Equality Act (2010) — prevents discrimination.

title: Computational Thinking
content:
🧠 Principles of Computational Thinking

flowchart TD
    CT["🧠 Computational Thinking"]:::title
    CT --> Dec["🔍 Decomposition
Break a problem into
smaller sub-problems"]:::blue
    CT --> Pat["🔁 Pattern Recognition
Spot similarities
between problems"]:::green
    CT --> Abs["🎯 Abstraction
Focus on what matters
ignore irrelevant detail"]:::amber
    CT --> Alg["📝 Algorithm Design
Create step-by-step
solutions"]:::purple

    classDef title fill:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3
    classDef blue fill:#2563eb,color:#ffffff
    classDef green fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    classDef amber fill:#d97706,color:#ffffff
    classDef purple fill:#9333ea,color:#ffffff

Computational thinking is a problem-solving method that uses concepts from computer science. It's not about programming — it's about how to think like a computer scientist.

💡 Remember: Computational thinking has four pillars: Decomposition (break into smaller problems), Pattern recognition (spot similarities), Abstraction (focus on important details, ignore irrelevant ones), and Algorithm design (create step-by-step solutions). These skills are tested across all programming questions.

Abstraction

Removing unnecessary detail to focus on the important parts. A map is a good example — it doesn't show every tree and lamppost, only the roads and landmarks you need for navigation.

In programming, abstraction means hiding complex implementation details behind simpler interfaces. When you use print() in Python, you don't need to know how the display driver works.

Decomposition

Breaking a complex problem down into smaller, more manageable parts. Each part can be solved separately, then the solutions are combined.

Example: Making a cup of tea can be decomposed into: boil water → warm teapot → add tea bag → pour water → steep → pour milk → serve.

Benefits: Each sub-problem is simpler to solve, problems can be worked on by different people, and sub-solutions can be reused.

Algorithmic Thinking

Creating a step-by-step set of rules to solve a problem. An algorithm is like a recipe — a clear sequence of instructions that, if followed correctly, always produces the right result.

Algorithmic thinking involves identifying the specific inputs, processes, and outputs needed for each part of the problem.

📌 Exam Tip: A 2-mark question: "Explain what is meant by abstraction." Answer: Abstraction is removing unnecessary detail from a problem so that only the essential features remain. For example, a sat-nav app shows roads but ignores building colours — the colour is irrelevant to navigation.

Computational Thinking — Unpack a Problem

Decomposition
Breaking down a complex problem into smaller, more manageable parts. Each part can be solved separately, making the overall problem easier to tackle.
Example: Making a cup of tea decomposes into: boil water, warm teapot, add teabag, pour water, steep, pour milk, remove teabag.

title: Designing, Creating and Refining Algorithms
content:
📥📤📊 Inputs, Processes, and Outputs

Every problem can be defined by its inputs (data that goes in), processes (what happens to the data), and outputs (the result). This is often called the IPO model.

flowchart LR
    I["📥 Input"]:::input --> P["⚙️ Process"]:::process
    P --> O["📤 Output"]:::output

    classDef input fill:#2563eb,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#ffffff
    classDef process fill:#16a34a,stroke:#15803d,color:#ffffff
    classDef output fill:#d97706,stroke:#b45309,color:#ffffff

📋 Structure Diagrams

Structure diagrams show the hierarchy of a problem — broken into sub-problems like an organisational chart. Each box represents a task, and lines show how they relate.

💡 Remember: Structure diagrams use top-down design — start with the big problem, keep splitting until each sub-problem is simple enough to code.

📝 Representing Algorithms

📄 Pseudocode

Writing algorithms in plain English-like statements that follow programming logic but don't use precise syntax. OCR has its own exam reference language (a form of pseudocode).

🔲 Flowcharts

Visual diagrams using standard symbols connected by arrows:

    IO["📋 Input / Output"]:::io
    PROC["⚙️ Process"]:::process
    DEC{"🔀 Decision?"}:::decision
    FLOW["➡️ Flow lines"]:::flow

    START --> IO
    IO --> PROC
    PROC --> DEC
    DEC -->|Yes ✅| FLOW
    DEC -->|No ❌| FLOW

title: Searching and Sorting Algorithms
content:
🔍 Searching Algorithms

Linear Search

Checks each item in the list one by one from start to end until the target is found (or the list ends).

💡 Key Comparison: Linear search checks every item one by one — simple but slow (O(n)). Binary search repeatedly halves the list — much faster (O(log n)) but the list must be sorted first. This trade-off is a classic exam topic.
  • Works on any list — doesn't need to be sorted
  • Simple to understand and implement
  • Slow on large lists — in the worst case, checks every item (e.g., finding Z in an A-Z list of 100,000 items)
flowchart LR
    LS1["🔍 Check item 1"] --> LS2{"Match?"}
    LS2 -->|"✅ Yes"| LS3["🎉 Found!"]
    LS2 -->|"❌ No"| LS4["🔍 Check next item"]
    LS4 --> LS5{"Match?"}
    LS5 -->|"✅ Yes"| LS6["🎉 Found!"]
    LS5 -->|"❌ No"| LS7["🔍 ... keep going ..."]
    LS7 --> LS8["😔 Not found
(reached end of list)"]

    classDef default fill:#2563eb,color:#ffffff

flowchart TD
    BS1["Sorted list:
[3, 5, 7, 9, 12]"] --> BS2{"Find middle:
7"}
    BS2 -->|"Target 9 > 7
Look right"| BS3["Search right half:
[9, 12]"]
    BS3 --> BS4{"Find middle:
9"}
    BS4 -->|"✅ Target = 9
Match!"| BS5["🎉 Found in
2 steps!"]
    BS2 -->|"Target < 7
Look left"| BS6["Would search
left half"]

    classDef default fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff

Binary Search

Repeatedly divides the sorted list in half until the target is found. Steps:

  1. Find the middle item of the sorted list
  2. If it matches the target — done!
  3. If the target is smaller, repeat with the left half
  4. If the target is larger, repeat with the right half

  • Requires: The list MUST be sorted first
  • Much faster than linear search — halves the search area each step

🔍 Sorting Algorithms

Bubble Sort

Repeatedly steps through the list, comparing adjacent items and swapping them if they're in the wrong order. With each pass, the largest item 'bubbles up' to its correct position.

Simple but slow — especially on large, unordered lists.

flowchart TD
    B1["Start:
[5, 3, 8, 1]"] --> B2["Compare 5 & 3
→ swap"]
    B2 --> B3["[3, 5, 8, 1]"]
    B3 --> B4["Compare 5 & 8
→ keep"]
    B4 --> B5["[3, 5, 8, 1]"]
    B5 --> B6["Compare 8 & 1
→ swap"]
    B6 --> B7["[3, 5, 1, 8] ✅
Largest bubbled up"]
    B7 --> B8["↻ Repeat passes
until sorted"]
    B8 --> B9["✅ [1, 3, 5, 8]"]

    classDef default fill:#d97706,color:#ffffff

flowchart TD
    M1["[5, 3, 8, 1, 6, 2]"] --> M2["Split in half"]
    M2 --> M3["[5, 3, 8]"]
    M2 --> M4["[1, 6, 2]"]
    M3 --> M5["[5]
[3, 8]"]
    M5 --> M6["[5]
[3]
[8]"]
    M4 --> M7["[1]
[6, 2]"]
    M7 --> M8["[1]
[6]
[2]"]
    M6 --> M9["Merge sorted →
[3, 5, 8]"]
    M8 --> M10["Merge sorted →
[1, 2, 6]"]
    M9 --> M11["✅ Merge all →
[1, 2, 3, 5, 6, 8]"]
    M10 --> M11

    classDef default fill:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff

Merge Sort

A 'divide and conquer' algorithm:

  1. Divide the list in half repeatedly until you have single items
  2. Merge pairs back together, putting items in the correct order as you go

Much faster than bubble sort on large lists, but uses more memory.

Insertion Sort

Builds the sorted list one item at a time. Takes each new item and inserts it into the correct position in the already-sorted part of the list.

Good for small or nearly-sorted lists.

Note for the exam: You need to understand the steps of each algorithm and be able to apply them to a data set, but you don't need to memorise the code for each algorithm.

📌 Exam Tip: A 4-mark question: "Compare linear search and binary search." Points to include: binary search is faster on large sorted datasets, but the data must be sorted first (which takes time). Linear search works on unsorted data and is simpler to implement.

title: Programming Fundamentals
content:
📦 Variables and Constants

  • 📦 Variable: A named memory location storing a value that can change during execution. score = 0
  • 🔒 Constant: A named memory location whose value cannot change once set. PI = 3.14159
💡 Remember: Use constants for values that never change (e.g., tax rate, conversion factor). This makes your code easier to maintain and less error-prone.

🔀 Basic Programming Constructs

1️⃣ Sequence

Instructions execute one after another, in order. The most basic form of program flow.

flowchart LR
    A[📥 INPUT score]:::io --> B[➕ score = score + 10]:::process
    B --> C[📤 OUTPUT score]:::output
    classDef io fill:#2563eb,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#ffffff
    classDef process fill:#16a34a,stroke:#15803d,color:#ffffff
    classDef output fill:#d97706,stroke:#b45309,color:#ffffff

2️⃣ Selection

The program makes a decision and chooses a path based on a condition. Uses IF, ELSE IF, ELSE.

flowchart TD
    COND{"🌡️ temperature > 30?"}:::decision
    COND -->|Yes ✅| HOT["📤 OUTPUT "It is hot today""]:::output
    COND -->|No ❌| COLD["📤 OUTPUT "It is not too hot""]:::output
    classDef decision fill:#d97706,stroke:#b45309,color:#ffffff
    classDef output fill:#059669,stroke:#047857,color:#ffffff

IF temperature > 30 THEN

💡 Key Insight: Choosing the correct data type is essential for robust programming. It ensures that the computer reserves the right amount of memory (e.g. 1 bit for Boolean, 32 bits for Integer) and allows the CPU to perform appropriate operations (e.g. arithmetic on Integers, concatenation on Strings).
  • Integer: Whole numbers (positive, negative, or zero) without any decimal parts. E.g., 42, −7, 0.
  • Real / Float: Numbers with decimal parts. Used for measurements and fractional values. E.g., 3.14, −0.5, 100.0.
  • Boolean: Only two possible values — TRUE or FALSE. Often stored as a single bit (1 or 0).
  • Character: A single letter, digit, punctuation mark, or control symbol. E.g., 'A', '7', '!'.
  • String: A collection or array of characters grouped together in a sequence. E.g., "Hello, world!".

Casting: Type Conversions

Casting is the process of explicitly converting a value from one data type to another. This is common when taking user input, which is read as a String by default, and converting it to an Integer or Real to carry out arithmetic calculations.

age_input = "15"             # Stored as a String
age = int(age_input)         # Cast to Integer
next_year = age + 1          # Arithmetic can now be done safely
print(next_year)             # Outputs: 16

⚖️ Choosing the Right Data Type

ScenarioSuitable Data TypeRationale
Number of students in a classIntegerCannot have half a student; requires whole numbers.
A person's height in metresReal/FloatRequires fractional accuracy (e.g. 1.75m).
Whether a light switch is on or offBooleanExactly two mutually exclusive states.
A telephone numberStringMust preserve leading zeros (e.g. 07700...) and space symbols.
A single letter grade (e.g., 'A')CharacterExactly one single letter or code.
📌 Exam Tip: A classic exam trick is asking what data type should be used for a telephone number. Always answer String, not Integer. Integers strip leading zeros and do not allow spaces or symbols (like '+').
⚠️ Common Mistake: Confusing the character `'5'` or string `"5"` with the integer `5`. You cannot perform calculations on text-based representations without casting them to numeric types first.

title: Additional Programming Techniques
content:
🛠️ String Manipulation

  • Concatenation: Joining strings together. E.g., "Hello" & " World" → "Hello World"
  • Slicing: Extracting a portion of a string. E.g., getting the first 3 characters: "Hello"[0:3] → "Hel"
  • Length: Finding the number of characters in a string

🛠️ File Handling

Basic operations: OPEN → READ/WRITE → CLOSE. Files must be opened before use and closed afterward to free up system resources.

💡 Key Techniques: File handling lets programs read from and write to external files (open → read/write → close). Records group related data under one name. SQL (Structured Query Language) is used to query databases with commands like SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE.

🛠️ Records (Structured Data)

A record is a collection of related fields grouped together. For example, a student record might contain: name, age, form class, and student ID number.

🛠️ SQL Queries

Structured Query Language for searching databases. Key commands:

  • SELECT — which fields to retrieve
  • FROM — which table to search
  • WHERE — conditions to filter results

Example: SELECT name, age FROM students WHERE form = '11A'

🛠️ Arrays

  • 1D array: A list of items of the same data type, accessed by index (position). E.g., scores[0], scores[1]
  • 2D array: A table with rows and columns. Used to store grids of data. E.g., grid[row][col]

🛠️ Sub Programs

  • Function: Returns a value. Used for calculations or data processing.
  • Procedure: Performs an action but doesn't return a value. Used for tasks like printing output.

Sub programs make code more organised, reusable, and easier to debug.

🛠️ Random Number Generation

Used in games, simulations, and sampling. E.g., RANDOM(1, 6) simulates rolling a die.

📌 Exam Tip: When writing file handling code in the exam, always remember the three steps: open the file (with mode r/w/a), read/write data, and close the file. Missing the close step means data may not be saved.

title: Defensive Design
content:
🛡️ Designing for Robustness

Robust programs handle unexpected inputs and situations gracefully without crashing or producing incorrect output.

    In --> Pres["📝 Presence Check
Was anything entered?"]:::blue
    Pres --> Range["📏 Range Check
Is it within valid limits?"]:::green
    Range --> Type["🔤 Type Check
Is it the right data type?"]:::amber
    Type --> Len["📐 Length Check
Is it the right length?"]:::purple
    Len --> Format["🔣 Format Check
Does it match a pattern?"]:::cyan
    Format --> OK["✅ Valid Input
Process normally"]:::ok
    Format --> Err["❌ Invalid Input
Show error, re-prompt"]:::err

    classDef blue fill:#2563eb,color:#ffffff
    classDef green fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    classDef amber fill:#d97706,color:#ffffff
    classDef purple fill:#9333ea,color:#ffffff
    classDef cyan fill:#0891b2,color:#ffffff
    classDef ok fill:#059669,color:#ffffff
    classDef err fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
💡 Remember: Anticipating misuse means designing your program to handle unexpected inputs gracefully. For example, if the user enters a letter where a number is expected, the program should show a helpful error message instead of crashing. This is called being defensive.

Anticipating Misuse

Programmers must consider what could go wrong and design for it. What if the user enters text where a number is expected? What if they click 'submit' without filling in a field? A robust program handles these cases.

Authentication

Verifying the identity of a user before granting access. Common methods include username and password, biometrics (fingerprint/face ID), and two-factor authentication.

Input Validation

Checking that input data meets certain rules before processing it. Types of validation:

  • Presence check: Has the user entered something? (Not left blank)
  • Range check: Is the value within an acceptable range? (e.g., age 0-120)
  • Type check: Is it the right data type? (e.g., number not text)
  • Length check: Is it the right length? (e.g., password minimum 8 chars)
  • Format check: Does it match a pattern? (e.g., email contains @)
  • Presence check: Is data present? (e.g., required field not empty)

Maintainability

Well-written code is easier for others (or your future self) to understand and modify.

  • Sub programs: Using procedures and functions to break code into logical sections
  • Naming conventions: Using meaningful variable names (e.g., studentAge not x)
  • Indentation: Properly aligning code to show structure
  • Commenting: Adding explanatory notes to explain complex or non-obvious parts of the code
📌 Exam Tip: A 5-mark question: "A program asks users to enter their age. Describe how you would validate this input." Include: range check (must be between e.g. 1-130), data type check (must be a number), presence check (cannot be blank), and what happens if validation fails (error message, re-prompt).

Defensive Design — Input Validation Tester

Try entering different values and see the validation rules in action:
Rules: Must be a number between 0 and 120.
Defensive Design Techniques: Input validation (check data is sensible), authentication (verify user identity), error handling (graceful failure), maintainability (comments, indentation, naming conventions).

title: Testing
content:
🧪 The Purpose of Testing

Testing ensures that a program works correctly, handles errors gracefully, and meets its requirements. It's an essential part of the development process.

        N["✅ Normal
Valid input
e.g., age = 25"]:::green
        B["🔲 Boundary
At edge of range
e.g., age = 0, 1, 130"]:::amber
        I["❌ Invalid
Wrong type
e.g., age = 'hello'"]:::red

Valid type but

    classDef amber fill:#d97706,color:#ffffff
    classDef red fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
    classDef purple fill:#9333ea,color:#ffffff
💡 Key Types of Test Data: Normal (valid, expected input — e.g., age 25), Boundary (at the edge of valid range — e.g., age 0, 1, 130 if range is 1-130), Invalid (wrong type — e.g., "hello" for age), Erroneous (unexpected but valid type — e.g., age -5). Testing all four types proves robustness.

Types of Testing

Iterative Testing

Testing is done during development — as each module or feature is completed, it's tested immediately. This means errors are found and fixed early, when they're cheaper and easier to resolve.

Final / Terminal Testing

The complete program is tested at the end of development to ensure all parts work together correctly. Uses a formal test plan with expected results and test data.

Types of Errors

  • Syntax error: Breaks the grammar rules of the programming language. The program won't run. E.g., missing bracket, misspelled keyword.
  • Logic error: The program runs but produces incorrect results. E.g., using '÷' instead of '+' in a total calculation.

Types of Test Data

TypeDescriptionExample (age, valid 0-120)
NormalData that should be accepted and processed correctly25, 0, 120
BoundaryData at the very edge of the acceptable range0 (lower), 120 (upper), or 119, 121 (just inside/outside)
Invalid/ErroneousData of the correct type that should be rejected−5 (age can't be negative)
ErroneousData of the wrong data type that should be rejected"hello" (text for age field)

Refining Algorithms

After testing, algorithms can be refined (improved) based on test results — fixing errors, improving efficiency, or adding missing functionality.

📌 Exam Tip: A 4-mark question: "Explain the difference between iterative testing and final/terminal testing." Iterative: testing during development — find and fix bugs as you go. Final: testing the complete program at the end — ensures it meets requirements before release.

Testing — Test Data Explorer

title: Boolean Logic
content:
🔣 Boolean Logic

Boolean logic uses True (1) and False (0) values. It's the foundation of all computer processing — every decision a computer makes reduces to Boolean logic.

💡 Key Insight: Computers use Boolean logic because the processor consists of billions of tiny transistors that act as switches, representing only two physical states: electrical current flowing (1 / True) or not flowing (0 / False).

🔌 The Three Basic Logic Gates

1️⃣ AND Gate

Output is True (1) only if both inputs are True.

flowchart LR
    A["A"]:::input --> AND["AND"]:::andgate
    B["B"]:::input --> AND
    AND --> Q["Q = A AND B"]:::output

    classDef input fill:#2563eb,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#ffffff
    classDef andgate fill:#16a34a,stroke:#15803d,color:#ffffff
    classDef output fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#6d28d9,color:#ffffff
ABQ = A AND B
000
010
100
11✅ 1

2️⃣ OR Gate

Output is True (1) if at least one input is True.

flowchart LR
    A["A"]:::input --> OR["OR"]:::_or
    B["B"]:::input --> OR
    OR --> Q["Q = A OR B"]:::output

    classDef input fill:#2563eb,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#ffffff
    classDef _or fill:#d97706,stroke:#b45309,color:#ffffff
    classDef output fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#6d28d9,color:#ffffff
ABQ = A OR B
000
01✅ 1
10✅ 1
11✅ 1

3️⃣ NOT Gate

Output is the opposite of the input (also called an inverter).

flowchart LR
    A["A"]:::input --> NOT["NOT"]:::notgate
    NOT --> Q["Q = NOT A"]:::output

    classDef input fill:#2563eb,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#ffffff
    classDef notgate fill:#dc2626,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff
    classDef output fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#6d28d9,color:#ffffff
AQ = NOT A
0✅ 1
10
💡 Easy Memory Trick: AND = both (like "I need A and B"). OR = either (like "tea or coffee"). NOT = opposite (like "NOT hungry").
📌 Exam Tip: You may be asked to draw logic gate circuits or complete truth tables. Always work gate by gate from inputs to output — don't try to do it all in one step.

🧠 Boolean Expressions & Symbols

You must recognize three different forms of logic notation for Boolean expressions:

  • Word Form: Q = NOT (A AND B)
  • Standard Symbolic Form: Q = ¬(A ∧ B) (where ¬ is NOT, and ∧ is AND).
  • Alternative algebraic symbols: Q = (A · B)' or Q = A · B (where dot · represents AND, plus + represents OR, and a bar over letters represents NOT).

title: Programming Languages
content:
💬 Levels of Programming Language

High-Level Languages

Closer to human language. One instruction in a high-level language translates to many machine code instructions.

flowchart LR
    subgraph Levels["💬 Language Levels"]
        HL["🐍 High-Level
Python, Java, C#
Easy to write & read
Needs translation"]:::high
        LL["⚙️ Low-Level
Assembly, Machine Code
Hard to write
Fast execution"]:::low
    end

    HL --> Comp["🔄 Compiler or Interpreter
Translates to machine code"]:::trans

    classDef high fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2
    classDef low fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2
    classDef trans fill:#2563eb,color:#ffffff
💡 Language Generations: Low-level languages (machine code, assembly) are closer to the hardware — fast but hard to write. High-level languages (Python, Java) are closer to human language — easier to write, debug, and maintain, but need translation to machine code. Most real-world programming uses high-level languages.
  • Easier to read, write, and debug
  • Uses English-like words (IF, WHILE, PRINT)
  • Portable — can run on different types of hardware
  • Examples: Python, Java, C#, JavaScript, PHP

Low-Level Languages

Closer to machine code. Each instruction typically corresponds to one machine code instruction.

  • Harder to read and write (uses numbers and symbols)
  • Very fast and efficient
  • Specific to a particular processor type
  • Used for device drivers, embedded systems, and operating system kernels
  • Examples: Assembly language, Machine code

Translators

High-level code must be translated into machine code for the computer to execute it.

Compiler: Translates the entire source code at once into an executable file. The executable can run without the compiler.

Interpreter: Translates and executes code line by line. Slower at runtime but easier to debug (stops at the first error).

FeatureCompilerInterpreter
TranslatesAll at onceLine by line
SpeedFaster executionSlower execution
Errors shownAll at endOne at a time (stops on first)
OutputExecutable file (.exe)No standalone file
📌 Exam Tip: A 3-mark question: "Explain the difference between a compiler and an interpreter." Compiler: translates the entire program at once, produces an executable file, faster execution. Interpreter: translates one line at a time, no executable file, easier debugging (stops at the error line).

Compiler vs Interpreter — Step Through

Source code -> Lexical analysis -> Syntax analysis -> Code generation -> Object code -> Executable file

🖥️ What is an IDE?

An Integrated Development Environment (IDE) is a software application that provides everything a programmer needs to write, test, and debug code — all in one place.

💡 Key IDE Features: Syntax highlighting colours code elements to make it readable. Error diagnostics highlights errors as you type. Breakpoints pause execution at a specific line for debugging. Variable watches show variable values during execution. These tools help you write and debug code faster.

IDE Tools and Facilities

Editor

Where you write your code. Modern editors include features like syntax highlighting (colour-coded keywords), line numbers, and auto-completion (suggesting code as you type).

Error Diagnostics

The IDE checks your code for errors as you type and highlights them. It can identify syntax errors (missing brackets, misspelled keywords) and sometimes logic issues. Error messages help you understand what's wrong.

Run-Time Environment

Allows you to run your program from within the IDE. You can test how the program behaves without leaving the development environment. Output is usually shown in a built-in console/terminal.

Translator

An IDE includes (or integrates with) a compiler or interpreter to translate your code. You can typically choose between compiling the whole program or running it through an interpreter for debugging.

How IDE Tools Help Development

  • Editor: Write code faster with auto-completion and syntax highlighting
  • Debugger: Step through code line by line, inspect variables, set breakpoints
  • Error diagnostics: Spot mistakes immediately rather than finding them at runtime
  • Run-time environment: Test quickly without switching between programs
📌 Exam Tip: A 3-mark question: "Describe how a breakpoint helps a programmer debug code." Answer: A breakpoint pauses program execution at a specific line. The programmer can then inspect variable values and step through code line by line to find where the logic goes wrong.

IDE Features Explorer

Editor
The code editor is where you write your program. IDEs provide a text editor with features like line numbers, indentation guides, and multiple tabs for working on different files simultaneously.

title: The Internet, DNS, and Protocols
content:
🌐 The Internet as a Network of Networks

The internet is a global network connecting millions of smaller networks. It's not the same as the World Wide Web — the web is just one service that runs on the internet (along with email, file transfer, VoIP, etc.).

💡 How DNS Works: When you type "google.com" in your browser, the DNS (Domain Name System) translates that domain name into an IP address (like 142.250.180.46). Think of DNS as the Internet's phonebook — you look up the name, it gives you the number.

IP Addressing

Every device connected to the internet needs a unique identifier called an IP address. There are two versions:

  • IPv4: 32-bit address written as four decimal numbers (e.g., 192.168.1.1). Provides ~4.3 billion addresses — nearly exhausted.
  • IPv6: 128-bit address written in hexadecimal (e.g., 2001:db8::ff00:42:8329). Provides vastly more addresses.

MAC Addresses vs IP Addresses

A MAC address is a permanent, unique identifier burned into a network interface card (NIC) at the factory. An IP address can change (dynamic) and identifies the device on a network.

  • MAC: physical, hardware-based, stays with the device
  • IP: logical, network-based, can change depending on the network

DNS — Domain Name System

DNS servers act like the internet's phonebook. They translate domain names (like www.bbc.co.uk) into IP addresses (like 151.101.192.81) that computers use to communicate.

When you type a URL, your device sends a DNS query, gets back the IP address, then uses that to connect to the web server.

sequenceDiagram
    participant B as 🌐 Browser
    participant D as 📖 DNS Server
    participant S as 🖥️ Web Server

    B->>D: "What is the IP for
google.com?"
    D->>D: 🔍 Look up in
DNS database
    D-->>B: "IP = 142.250.180.46"
    B->>S: "Connect to
142.250.180.46"
    S-->>B: "📄 Here's the webpage!"

The TCP/IP Model

The TCP/IP model organises network communication into 4 layers. Each layer handles a different aspect:

  1. Application: High-level protocols for specific services (HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, SMTP, POP3, IMAP)
  2. Transport: Ensures reliable delivery of data (TCP) or fast delivery (UDP)
  3. Internet: Handles addressing and routing packets across networks (IP)
  4. Link: Physical transmission over hardware (Ethernet, Wi-Fi)
flowchart TD
    subgraph TCPIP["📶 TCP/IP 4-Layer Model"]
        direction TB
        L4["📱 Application Layer
HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, SMTP
What the user interacts with"]:::app
        L3["📦 Transport Layer
TCP (reliable) or UDP (fast)
Ensures data arrives correctly"]:::trans
        L2["🌍 Internet Layer
IP addressing & routing
Finds the best path across networks"]:::net
        L1["🔌 Link Layer
Ethernet, Wi-Fi
Physical transmission of data"]:::link

        L4 --> L3
        L3 --> L2
        L2 --> L1
    end

    classDef app fill:#2563eb,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2
    classDef trans fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2
    classDef net fill:#d97706,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2
    classDef link fill:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2

Key Protocols and Their Purposes

ProtocolFull NamePurpose
HTTPHyperText Transfer ProtocolTransfer web pages
HTTPSHTTP SecureEncrypted web page transfer
FTPFile Transfer ProtocolTransfer files between computers
SMTPSimple Mail Transfer ProtocolSend emails (outgoing)
POP3Post Office Protocol v3Receive emails (download to device)
IMAPInternet Message Access ProtocolReceive emails (keep on server)

Why Layers Matter

Using a layered model means each layer can be developed, updated, or replaced independently — as long as it provides the expected service to the layer above. For example, you can switch from Ethernet to Wi-Fi at the Link layer without changing your web browser (Application layer).

📌 Exam Tip: A 4-mark question: "Explain how data is sent across the Internet using packet switching." Key points: data is split into packets, each packet takes the best available route (may be different routes), packets are reassembled at the destination, missing packets are re-requested.

DNS Lookup — How a URL Becomes an IP

www.example.com
How DNS Works: Your browser asks a DNS server what IP address corresponds to a domain name. The DNS server looks it up and returns the IP (e.g., 93.184.216.34). Your browser then connects to that IP address.

title: Representing Text, Images and Sound
content:
🔤 Representing Different Types of Data

Computers store everything as binary (0s and 1s). Different types of data need different encoding schemes to represent them.

flowchart LR
    Text["🔤 Text
ASCII = 7 bits/char
Unicode = up to 32 bits"]:::blue
    Img["🖼️ Images
Grid of pixels
Colour depth × Resolution"]:::green
    Snd["🔊 Sound
Sample rate × Bit depth
Analogue → Digital samples"]:::amber

    classDef blue fill:#2563eb,color:#ffffff
    classDef green fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    classDef amber fill:#d97706,color:#ffffff
💡 Key Formulas to Memorise: Image file size = colour depth (bits/pixel) × width × height. Sound file size = sample rate × duration × bit depth. Text file size = bits per character × number of characters. Know these for calculation questions!

Character Encoding: ASCII and Unicode

ASCII (American Standard Code for Information Interchange) uses 7 bits to represent 128 characters — including letters (A-Z, a-z), digits (0-9), punctuation, and control codes.

Unicode extends ASCII to support characters from virtually all languages. It uses up to 32 bits per character, allowing over a million different characters including emojis.

Representing Images

Digital images are made of tiny squares called pixels (picture elements).

  • Resolution: The number of pixels in the image (e.g., 1920x1080). More pixels = higher resolution = larger file size.
  • Colour depth: The number of bits used to store each pixel's colour. 1 bit = black & white, 8 bits = 256 colours, 24 bits = 16.7 million colours (true colour).
  • File size = width x height x colour depth (in bits).

Representing Sound

Sound is a continuous analogue wave. To store it digitally, the computer takes samples at regular intervals:

  • Sample rate: Number of samples taken per second (measured in Hz). Higher sample rate = more accurate = larger file.
  • Bit depth: Number of bits per sample. Higher bit depth = more precise measurement = larger file.

Compression

Compression reduces file sizes. There are two types:

  • Lossy compression: Permanently removes some data to reduce file size. The quality is reduced but hopefully not noticeably (e.g., JPEG images, MP3 audio). Cannot be reversed.
  • Lossless compression: Reduces file size without losing any data. The original can be perfectly reconstructed (e.g., PNG images, ZIP files). Used when accuracy matters.
📌 Exam Tip: A 2-mark question: "Explain the effect of increasing the sample rate when recording sound." Answer: A higher sample rate means more samples per second, producing better quality sound reproduction but creating a larger file size. It is a trade-off between quality and storage space.
